title: Subarachnoid Haemorrhage
oneLiner: Bleeding into subarachnoid space, usually from ruptured aneurysm; sudden severe headache that can kill within hours if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Re-bleeding** – highest risk within first 6-12 hours; single finding: sudden drop in GCS + new severe headache after initial improvement
- **Acute hydrocephalus** – obstructive hydrocephalus from blood clots; single finding: deteriorating consciousness + enlarged ventricles on CT
- **Raised ICP (Cushing’s reflex)** – bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing; treat as impending herniation
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Thunderclap headache (maximal intensity within seconds) — “worst headache of life”
  - Collapse / loss of consciousness (may be transient)
  - Nausea & vomiting
  - Photophobia, neck stiffness
  - Seizure at onset (10-20%)
  - Focal neurological deficit (e.g. third nerve palsy – posterior communicating artery aneurysm)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - GCS (use as baseline – any drop is critical)
  - Meningism: neck stiffness, Kernig’s sign
  - Retinal haemorrhage (Terson syndrome) – fundoscopy
  - Cranial nerve signs (CN III palsy, CN VI palsy)
  - Hemiparesis (if mass effect / vasospasm)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Migraine (especially thunderclap onset)** – but typically recurrent, no neck stiffness, no blood on CT/LP
- **Bacterial meningitis** – fever, meningism, prodromal symptoms; CSF shows low glucose, high protein, neutrophils
- **Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis** – headache gradual, may have papilloedema, protein C/S deficiency, post-partum
- **Benign thunderclap headache** – no blood, no aneurysm, but CT/LP mandatory to exclude
  
**Splitter:** CT head non-contrast shows hyperdense blood in subarachnoid spaces → diagnosis confirmed; if CT negative → LP for xanthochromia.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - GCS (ongoing monitoring)
  - Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia mimics)
  - ECG (look for neurogenic T-wave inversions – Takotsubo-like)
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, coagulation, LFTs, CRP, troponin
  - Blood gas (lactate, pCO₂ – no hypercapnia)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Non-contrast CT head – best within 6 hours of onset (sensitivity ~98% within 6h, drops to ~50% after 24h)
  - **Second-line:** CT angiogram (if CT positive ± CTA to locate aneurysm); LP for xanthochromia (if CT negative but clinical suspicion high)
  - **Special tests:** LP – measure opening pressure, send for cell count, protein, glucose, culture; xanthochromia (bilirubin) requires spectrophotometry, performed ≥12 hours after onset
- **Never-miss test:** LP after negative CT head if suspicion remains – missing subarachnoid haemorrhage is a catastrophic error
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - ABC – secure airway if GCS ≤8 or dropping
  - Position: flat (slight head elevation if raised ICP)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol IV – avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk)
  - Antiemetic: ondansetron IV (avoid metoclopramide – may lower seizure threshold)
  - Nimodipine: 60mg orally 4-hourly for vasospasm prophylaxis (start as soon as diagnosis confirmed)
  - Discuss with neurosurgery / regional centre (all aneurysmal SAH) for possible transfer
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - BP control: maintain systolic <160 mmHg (permissive hypotension) – use labetalol IV or nicardipine IV – [check local policy for target]
  - Nimodipine 60mg PO 4-hourly for 21 days (or via NG if unable to swallow)
  - Fluid balance: aim euvolemia – give 0.9% saline IV 1.5-3L/day (avoid hypotonic fluids)
  - Avoid hypovolaemia (worsens vasospasm)
  - Seizure prophylaxis not routine – treat only if seizure occurs
  - Monitor for vasospasm (day 4-21): new focal neuro, fluctuating GCS → CT angiogram/perfusion
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive aneurysm treatment: endovascular coiling (preferred) or surgical clipping
  - Follow-up CTA/DSA for angiogram-negative SAH
  - Outpatient support: neuro-rehabilitation, cognitive assessment
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline 1L/8 hours (adjust to urine output)
  - VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (TED stockings ± intermittent pneumatic compression) – no chemical prophylaxis until aneurysm secured (check local policy)
  - Diet: NBM until neurosurgery review (risk of coiling/clipping)
  - Observations: 1-2 hourly neuro obs (GCS, pupil, limb power) + NEWS2
  - Nimodipine charted PO 4-hourly – do not stop abruptly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Nimodipine** – 60mg PO 4-hourly for 21 days (first-line for vasospasm prevention)
- **Labetalol** – 20-80mg IV bolus (for acute hypertension if needed) – [check local policy for infusion]
- **Paracetamol** – 1g IV/PO 4-6 hourly as needed
- **Ondansetron** – 4-8mg IV/PO 8 hourly
- **Key contraindications:** Nimodipine – avoid in severe hypotension; labetalol – avoid in asthma, bradycardia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Nimodipine is metabolised in liver – use with caution in cirrhosis; no renal dose adjustment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Nimodipine omitted or stopped early** – delayed cerebral ischaemia from vasospasm is a leading cause of poor outcome
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Rebleeding (highest risk first 12h, especially if aneurysm unsecured)
  - Acute hydrocephalus (EVD required)
  - Vasospasm (day 4-21) → delayed cerebral ischaemia → infarction
  - Seizures
  - Neurogenic pulmonary oedema (rare, treat with supportive care)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cognitive impairment (memory, executive function)
  - Hydrocephalus (chronic, may require VP shunt)
  - Depression, fatigue
  - Hyponatraemia (SIADH or cerebral salt wasting – treat with hypertonic saline if symptomatic)
  - Epilepsy
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality ~25-30% within first 30 days; rebleeding mortality >70%
- Of survivors: ~50% have significant disability (dependent)
- Good outcome more likely in young patients, good-grade at presentation (WFNS 1-2), early aneurysm securement
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Start nimodipine as soon as SAH confirmed** – do not wait for neurosurgery to give first dose; it reduces vasospasm risk.
2. **Check GCS hourly** and document any drop (≥2 points) – immediate call to neuro team and consider repeat CT.
3. **Measure and document opening pressure on LP** – >20cm H₂O suggests hydrocephalus.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Do not use anti-platelet or anticoagulant drugs** until aneurysm secured (increases rebleed risk).
2. **Do not skip LP after a negative CT** if clinical suspicion remains – CT after 24h can miss SAH.
3. **Do not over-treat hypertension** – aggressive BP lowering (e.g. systolic <100) causes hypoperfusion and worsens vasospasm; target 160/90 unless otherwise specified.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New severe headache different from initial presentation
  - Worsening vision, double vision, or stiff neck
  - New focal weakness, speech difficulty, or confusion
- **Follow-up:**
  - Neurosurgery outpatient clinic (dates per discharge summary)
  - Angiogram follow-up if aneurysm not found on first scan
  - GP review in 1-2 weeks for BP check and mood assessment
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / neuro team immediately if:**
  - GCS drop ≥2 points from baseline (exclude sedation, new CT)
  - New focal neurological deficit (pupil change, hemiparesis)
  - Seizure
  - Deteriorating consciousness + bradycardia/hypertension (Cushing’s = impending herniation)
- **ICU referral:**
  - GCS ≤8 (airway protection)
  - Worsening respiratory status / need for ventilation
  - Haemodynamic instability after aneurysm securement
- **Thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red parameter: SpO₂ <91%, new hypotension <90 systolic)
  - GCS drop from 15 to ≤13
  - Rise in ICP (if EVD in situ, pressure >20mmHg sustained)

rawMarkdown:
## Subarachnoid Haemorrhage

**One-liner:** Bleeding into subarachnoid space, usually from ruptured aneurysm; sudden severe headache that can kill within hours if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Re-bleeding** – highest risk within first 6-12 hours; single finding: sudden drop in GCS + new severe headache after initial improvement
- **Acute hydrocephalus** – obstructive hydrocephalus from blood clots; single finding: deteriorating consciousness + enlarged ventricles on CT
- **Raised ICP (Cushing’s reflex)** – bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing; treat as impending herniation

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Thunderclap headache (maximal intensity within seconds) — “worst headache of life”
  - Collapse / loss of consciousness (may be transient)
  - Nausea & vomiting
  - Photophobia, neck stiffness
  - Seizure at onset (10-20%)
  - Focal neurological deficit (e.g. third nerve palsy – posterior communicating artery aneurysm)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - GCS (use as baseline – any drop is critical)
  - Meningism: neck stiffness, Kernig’s sign
  - Retinal haemorrhage (Terson syndrome) – fundoscopy
  - Cranial nerve signs (CN III palsy, CN VI palsy)
  - Hemiparesis (if mass effect / vasospasm)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Migraine (especially thunderclap onset)** – but typically recurrent, no neck stiffness, no blood on CT/LP
- **Bacterial meningitis** – fever, meningism, prodromal symptoms; CSF shows low glucose, high protein, neutrophils
- **Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis** – headache gradual, may have papilloedema, protein C/S deficiency, post-partum
- **Benign thunderclap headache** – no blood, no aneurysm, but CT/LP mandatory to exclude
  
**Splitter:** CT head non-contrast shows hyperdense blood in subarachnoid spaces → diagnosis confirmed; if CT negative → LP for xanthochromia.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - GCS (ongoing monitoring)
  - Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia mimics)
  - ECG (look for neurogenic T-wave inversions – Takotsubo-like)
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, coagulation, LFTs, CRP, troponin
  - Blood gas (lactate, pCO₂ – no hypercapnia)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Non-contrast CT head – best within 6 hours of onset (sensitivity ~98% within 6h, drops to ~50% after 24h)
  - **Second-line:** CT angiogram (if CT positive ± CTA to locate aneurysm); LP for xanthochromia (if CT negative but clinical suspicion high)
  - **Special tests:** LP – measure opening pressure, send for cell count, protein, glucose, culture; xanthochromia (bilirubin) requires spectrophotometry, performed ≥12 hours after onset
- **Never-miss test:** LP after negative CT head if suspicion remains – missing subarachnoid haemorrhage is a catastrophic error

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - ABC – secure airway if GCS ≤8 or dropping
  - Position: flat (slight head elevation if raised ICP)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol IV – avoid NSAIDs (bleeding risk)
  - Antiemetic: ondansetron IV (avoid metoclopramide – may lower seizure threshold)
  - Nimodipine: 60mg orally 4-hourly for vasospasm prophylaxis (start as soon as diagnosis confirmed)
  - Discuss with neurosurgery / regional centre (all aneurysmal SAH) for possible transfer
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - BP control: maintain systolic <160 mmHg (permissive hypotension) – use labetalol IV or nicardipine IV – [check local policy for target]
  - Nimodipine 60mg PO 4-hourly for 21 days (or via NG if unable to swallow)
  - Fluid balance: aim euvolemia – give 0.9% saline IV 1.5-3L/day (avoid hypotonic fluids)
  - Avoid hypovolaemia (worsens vasospasm)
  - Seizure prophylaxis not routine – treat only if seizure occurs
  - Monitor for vasospasm (day 4-21): new focal neuro, fluctuating GCS → CT angiogram/perfusion
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Definitive aneurysm treatment: endovascular coiling (preferred) or surgical clipping
  - Follow-up CTA/DSA for angiogram-negative SAH
  - Outpatient support: neuro-rehabilitation, cognitive assessment
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline 1L/8 hours (adjust to urine output)
  - VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (TED stockings ± intermittent pneumatic compression) – no chemical prophylaxis until aneurysm secured (check local policy)
  - Diet: NBM until neurosurgery review (risk of coiling/clipping)
  - Observations: 1-2 hourly neuro obs (GCS, pupil, limb power) + NEWS2
  - Nimodipine charted PO 4-hourly – do not stop abruptly

### Drugs & Doses
- **Nimodipine** – 60mg PO 4-hourly for 21 days (first-line for vasospasm prevention)
- **Labetalol** – 20-80mg IV bolus (for acute hypertension if needed) – [check local policy for infusion]
- **Paracetamol** – 1g IV/PO 4-6 hourly as needed
- **Ondansetron** – 4-8mg IV/PO 8 hourly
- **Key contraindications:** Nimodipine – avoid in severe hypotension; labetalol – avoid in asthma, bradycardia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Nimodipine is metabolised in liver – use with caution in cirrhosis; no renal dose adjustment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Nimodipine omitted or stopped early** – delayed cerebral ischaemia from vasospasm is a leading cause of poor outcome

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Rebleeding (highest risk first 12h, especially if aneurysm unsecured)
  - Acute hydrocephalus (EVD required)
  - Vasospasm (day 4-21) → delayed cerebral ischaemia → infarction
  - Seizures
  - Neurogenic pulmonary oedema (rare, treat with supportive care)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cognitive impairment (memory, executive function)
  - Hydrocephalus (chronic, may require VP shunt)
  - Depression, fatigue
  - Hyponatraemia (SIADH or cerebral salt wasting – treat with hypertonic saline if symptomatic)
  - Epilepsy

### Prognosis
- Mortality ~25-30% within first 30 days; rebleeding mortality >70%
- Of survivors: ~50% have significant disability (dependent)
- Good outcome more likely in young patients, good-grade at presentation (WFNS 1-2), early aneurysm securement

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Start nimodipine as soon as SAH confirmed** – do not wait for neurosurgery to give first dose; it reduces vasospasm risk.
2. **Check GCS hourly** and document any drop (≥2 points) – immediate call to neuro team and consider repeat CT.
3. **Measure and document opening pressure on LP** – >20cm H₂O suggests hydrocephalus.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Do not use anti-platelet or anticoagulant drugs** until aneurysm secured (increases rebleed risk).
2. **Do not skip LP after a negative CT** if clinical suspicion remains – CT after 24h can miss SAH.
3. **Do not over-treat hypertension** – aggressive BP lowering (e.g. systolic <100) causes hypoperfusion and worsens vasospasm; target 160/90 unless otherwise specified.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New severe headache different from initial presentation
  - Worsening vision, double vision, or stiff neck
  - New focal weakness, speech difficulty, or confusion
- **Follow-up:**
  - Neurosurgery outpatient clinic (dates per discharge summary)
  - Angiogram follow-up if aneurysm not found on first scan
  - GP review in 1-2 weeks for BP check and mood assessment

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / neuro team immediately if:**
  - GCS drop ≥2 points from baseline (exclude sedation, new CT)
  - New focal neurological deficit (pupil change, hemiparesis)
  - Seizure
  - Deteriorating consciousness + bradycardia/hypertension (Cushing’s = impending herniation)
- **ICU referral:**
  - GCS ≤8 (airway protection)
  - Worsening respiratory status / need for ventilation
  - Haemodynamic instability after aneurysm securement
- **Thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red parameter: SpO₂ <91%, new hypotension <90 systolic)
  - GCS drop from 15 to ≤13
  - Rise in ICP (if EVD in situ, pressure >20mmHg sustained)
